name: Navajo Nation (Naabeehó Bináhásdzo)
summary: The Navajo Nation (Naabeehó Bináhásdzo in Navajo, meaning 'Navajo Land') is a North American mixed common-law/Navajo-customary-law sovereign Indian Nation operating within the federal-trust framework of the United States — structurally distinctive globally as the largest US Indian tribal nation by land area (~71,000 km² spanning portions of Arizona, New Mexico, and Utah, with sovereignty over an additional ~46,500 km² of disputed land), as the largest US Indian tribal nation by population (~399,494 enrolled members per 2020 census), as the only US Indian tribal nation operating a comprehensive Navajo Common Law (Diné bi beenahaz'áanii) framework applied by the Navajo Nation Supreme Court under explicit constitutional preference over common law (Navajo Nation Code Title 7 § 204(B) — courts shall apply Navajo Common Law as the law of preference), and as the only US Indian tribal nation with its own constitutionally-protected Peacemaker Court (Hózhóójí Naat'aanii) restorative-justice framework operating parallel to the Anglo-American adversarial court framework. The Navajo Nation operates under the Treaty of Bosque Redondo (Treaty Between the United States of America and the Navajo Tribe of Indians, 1 June 1868), the Indian Reorganization Act 1934, the Indian Civil Rights Act 1968, the Indian Child Welfare Act 1978 (ICWA), and the federal Tribal Self-Governance Act 1994 + Tribal Law and Order Act 2010 + Violence Against Women Act 2013 (VAWA) frameworks. Family-law framework operates under the Navajo Nation Code Title 9 (Domestic Relations) with Navajo Common Law principles including the matrilineal clan-membership framework (k'éí), the Navajo concept of hózhǫ́ (harmony/beauty/balance) as guiding family-law principle, and Peacemaker Court restorative-justice processes. Parental authority operates under Navajo Nation Code Title 9 and ICWA federal framework. The Navajo Nation Supreme Court is the apex tribal court for civil and tribal matters; jurisdiction over criminal matters involving non-tribal members and major-crimes federal-court jurisdiction operates under the federal Major Crimes Act 1885. The Navajo Nation is silent on 'parental alienation' as a statutory label, though Navajo Common Law principles substantively address related family-discord frameworks via Peacemaker Court processes. The US is a Hague Convention 1980 party — Navajo Nation Hague applicability via US federal extension with ICWA-applicable additional federal framework.

statutory_framework.title: Treaty Between the United States and the Navajo Tribe of Indians
statutory_framework.relevance: International treaty of 1 June 1868 between United States and Navajo Tribe — foundational treaty establishing Navajo Nation reservation and sovereignty framework following the Long Walk.

statutory_framework.citation: Navajo Nation Code Title 7 § 204(B) (Navajo Common Law)
statutory_framework.title: Navajo Nation Code Title 7 — Navajo Common Law
statutory_framework.relevance: Navajo Nation Code provision establishing courts shall apply Navajo Common Law as the law of preference.

statutory_framework.title: Navajo Nation Code Title 9 — Domestic Relations
statutory_framework.relevance: Navajo Nation Code provisions governing family-law matters including parental responsibility and child custody.

statutory_framework.title: Indian Child Welfare Act
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal US Act of 8 November 1978 establishing federal framework for placement of Indian children in foster care and adoption proceedings.

statutory_framework.title: Indian Civil Rights Act
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal US Act of 11 April 1968 establishing constitutional rights protections for Indian tribal members.

apex_courts.name: Navajo Nation Supreme Court
apex_courts.role: Apex tribal court for civil and tribal matters from Navajo Nation.

apex_courts.name: Navajo Nation District Courts (Peacemaker Court / Hózhóójí Naat'aanii)
apex_courts.role: Tribal courts including Peacemaker Court restorative-justice framework operating parallel to the Anglo-American adversarial court framework.

apex_courts.name: United States Supreme Court (federal review of major-crimes and federal-Indian-law questions)
apex_courts.role: Federal final appellate court for federal-Indian-law questions from Navajo Nation.

professional_regulators.name: Navajo Nation Division of Health
professional_regulators.role: Navajo Nation health framework — operates under federal Indian Health Service framework.

anonymisation_convention: Navajo Nation family-court decisions are anonymised per Navajo Nation Supreme Court practice using initials.

key_developments.title: Long Walk of the Navajo
key_developments.description: Long Walk of the Navajo (Hwéeldi) of 1864 — forced relocation of ~8,500 Navajo from Diné Bikéyah to Bosque Redondo (Fort Sumner) under US Army Brigadier General James H. Carleton + Kit Carson framework — foundational historical injustice preceding 1868 Treaty + ~2,000 Navajo deaths during the Long Walk + foundation for subsequent 1868 Treaty of Bosque Redondo framework.

key_developments.title: Treaty of Bosque Redondo
key_developments.description: International treaty of 1 June 1868 between United States and Navajo Tribe — establishing Navajo Nation reservation + structurally distinctive globally one of few Indian treaties allowing tribal return to ancestral lands rather than relocation + foundation for subsequent Navajo Nation sovereignty framework + 156-year continuous treaty framework foundation.

key_developments.title: Navajo Tribal Council establishment + oil-discovery framework
key_developments.description: Navajo Tribal Council established 7 July 1923 following oil discoveries on Navajo reservation 1921 + significant pre-modern tribal governance framework foundation + foundation for subsequent 1934 Indian Reorganization Act decline framework + structurally distinctive globally first US Indian Council framework directly responsive to natural-resource-extraction context.

key_developments.title: Indian Reorganization Act
key_developments.description: Federal US Act of 18 June 1934 reorganising Indian tribal governance framework — Navajo Nation declined IRA constitution adoption, retaining traditional governance + pivotal modern Indian tribal self-determination framework foundation + significant context shaping subsequent Navajo Nation governance trajectory through 1968 + 1978 frameworks.

key_developments.title: Indian Civil Rights Act
key_developments.description: Federal US Act of 11 April 1968 establishing constitutional rights protections for Indian tribal members + pivotal modern framework cementing federal-tribal-civil-rights framework + foundation for subsequent 1978 ICWA + 1990 Peacemaker Court formal establishment framework.

key_developments.title: Indian Child Welfare Act (ICWA)
key_developments.description: Federal US Act of 8 November 1978 establishing federal framework for placement of Indian children in foster care and adoption proceedings + pivotal modern federal-Indian-child-welfare framework + foundation for subsequent 2023 Brackeen v Haaland Supreme Court framework upholding ICWA + structurally distinctive globally only modern federal Indigenous-child-welfare framework.

key_developments.title: Navajo Nation Code Title 7 § 204(B) Navajo Common Law
key_developments.description: Navajo Nation Code provision establishing courts shall apply Navajo Common Law as the law of preference — structurally distinctive globally only US Indian tribal nation operating comprehensive Navajo Common Law framework applied by tribal court under explicit constitutional preference over Anglo-American common law + foundation for subsequent 1990 Peacemaker Court framework.

key_developments.title: Peacemaker Court (Hózhóójí Naat'aanii) formal establishment
key_developments.description: Peacemaker Court formal establishment within Navajo Nation court system 1 April 1991 — restorative-justice framework operating parallel to adversarial court framework + structurally distinctive globally only US Indian tribal nation with constitutionally-protected Peacemaker Court restorative-justice framework + hózhǫ́ (harmony/beauty/balance) framework foundation.

key_developments.title: Violence Against Women Act (VAWA) tribal jurisdiction expansion
key_developments.description: Federal US Violence Against Women Reauthorization Act 2013 expanding tribal-court criminal jurisdiction over non-Indian domestic violence offenders on tribal lands — pivotal modern federal Indian tribal sovereignty restoration framework + foundation for subsequent 2022 VAWA Reauthorization Act expansion framework + significant context for Navajo Nation tribal court jurisdiction expansion.

key_developments.title: Buu Nygren Government + Navajo Nation Council + Brackeen-v-Haaland-ICWA-upheld framework
key_developments.description: Buu Nygren (Democratic) Navajo Nation President framework January 2023-onwards with Navajo Nation Council institutional positions addressing PA-related issues within Navajo Nation Code Title 9 (Domestic Relations) + Navajo Common Law Title 7 § 204(B) framework applied via Navajo Nation Supreme Court + Peacemaker Court. Brackeen v Haaland 599 U.S. 255 (15 June 2023) Supreme Court 7-2 decision upholding ICWA substantively significant institutional context. Substantively significant North American sovereign-Indian-Nation institutional jurisprudential development.

key_developments.title: Continuing Brackeen v Haaland framework + Navajo Nation self-determination
key_developments.description: Continuing Navajo Nation framework 2024 — continuing 2023 Brackeen v Haaland Supreme Court 7-2 decision upholding ICWA framework + continuing Navajo Common Law framework + continuing Peacemaker Court framework + continuing hózhǫ́ family-law framework + ongoing federal-tribal-trust-relationship framework + 156 years since 1868 Treaty of Bosque Redondo framework foundation.

structural_findings: Navajo Nation operates a mixed Anglo-American common-law + Navajo Common Law (Diné bi beenahaz'áanii) framework — places Navajo Nation in the North American sovereign-Indian-Nation cluster.
Largest US Indian tribal nation by land area (~71,000 km²) is structurally distinctive globally.
Largest US Indian tribal nation by population (~399,494 enrolled members) is structurally distinctive globally.

Only US Indian tribal nation operating comprehensive Navajo Common Law framework applied by tribal court under explicit constitutional preference over common law is structurally distinctive globally — Navajo Nation Code Title 7 § 204(B).

Only US Indian tribal nation with constitutionally-protected Peacemaker Court (Hózhóójí Naat'aanii) restorative-justice framework operating parallel to Anglo-American adversarial court framework is structurally distinctive globally.
Matrilineal clan-membership framework (k'éí) is structurally distinctive globally — only US Indian tribal nation with constitutionally-protected matrilineal clan-membership framework.
Hózhǫ́ (harmony/beauty/balance) as guiding family-law principle is structurally distinctive globally — only Indigenous-language family-law principle constitutionally entrenched.
Treaty of Bosque Redondo 1868 foundational framework is structurally distinctive within US Indian-treaty cluster.
Federal US Hague Convention 1980 + ICWA + Navajo Nation tribal framework intersection is structurally distinctive.
editorial_notes: Navajo Nation jurisdiction sidecar v1.1 — mixed Anglo-American common-law/Navajo Common Law sovereign Indian Nation within US federal-trust framework (1864 Long Walk + 1868 Treaty of Bosque Redondo + 1923 Navajo Tribal Council establishment + 1934 IRA decline + 1968 Indian Civil Rights Act + 1978 ICWA + 1985 Navajo Common Law Title 7 § 204(B) + 1990 Peacemaker Court + 2013 VAWA tribal jurisdiction expansion + 2024 continuing Brackeen v Haaland framework). Largest US Indian tribal nation by land area and population globally + only US Indian tribal nation operating comprehensive Navajo Common Law framework under explicit constitutional preference over common law + only US Indian tribal nation with constitutionally-protected Peacemaker Court restorative-justice framework + only US Indian tribal nation with constitutionally-protected matrilineal clan-membership framework + only Indigenous-language family-law principle (hózhǫ́) constitutionally entrenched + 156 years since 1868 Treaty of Bosque Redondo framework.
PA-recognition: silent statutory + no-apex-position + silent regulator.

Joins North-American + mixed Anglo-American-common-law/Navajo-Common-Law + sovereign-Indian-Nation cluster + largest-US-Indian-tribal-nation-globally-distinctive + Navajo-Common-Law-constitutional-preference + Peacemaker-Court-Hózhóójí-Naat'aanii-restorative-justice + matrilineal-clan-membership-k'éí + hózhǫ́-Indigenous-language-family-law-principle + Treaty-of-Bosque-Redondo-1868 + ICWA-federal-framework + Federal-US-Hague-extension clusters within the corpus.
